passages.title: Is a University Degree Necessary for Success?
passages.introHtml:
Discuss the following questions before you read.

  1. Why do you study at a university?
  2. What are the benefits of having a university degree?
passages.passageHtml:
The debate about whether one needs a degree to be successful versus the possibility of being successful by following one's passions is one that takes place all around the world. Basically, there are hundreds of thousands of people with high qualifications who are not able to get a job or become financially successful. There are also hundreds of examples of people who never finished college but built up successful businesses and who achieved vast financial wealth. Bill Gates started Microsoft when he was 19 and has become one of the world's wealthiest people. Mark Zuckerberg owns one of the most highly-valued technology companies in the U.S. Steve Jobs founded Apple in his garage. What do these three people have in common? None of them finished college; in fact, Bill Gates is considered "Harvard's most successful dropout." So, is it possible to succeed without college?

If people are willing to work harder than others, arrive at work first and leave last, and if they are willing to put in the time and effort to improve, there is no doubt that they will succeed. It may even take years to achieve success through their own endeavors, but if they never give up and if they keep learning from their mistakes, they will succeed and achieve what most people only dream of.

However, those who do not have much ambition, or those who do not like to take initiative and only put in the minimum required amount of effort at the things they do are different. Following one's own passions and business ideas is a lot riskier, but at the same time if a person knows that he is going to give everything he has and more in terms of effort, time, and money, then he has higher chances of succeeding financially. If they are the kind of people who need to be told what to do, they will probably never start their own business or earn a lot of money. Even if they start it, they will probably lose focus and never experience the success of their actions. Then a degree will theoretically allow these kinds of people to get a decent job and provide them with financial security as long as they can work.

As a result, people do not need a university degree to achieve financial success in life, and they definitely do not need one in order to lead a happy life. Getting a degree is a safer bet for financial security, but also not a guarantee for their future.

The definition of success varies from person to person. For some it may be having enough money. For some, it simply means having a loving and supportive family. Regardless of how you personally define success, you should just keep in mind that different people lead different lives and there is not one single way of being successful in life. Some may not have a desire to be independent and may prefer to have a job and work for a company with a university degree, but it does not mean that it will assure them of success in the future. Some may also pursue their passions and business ideas without having a university degree and these may bring them huge success in their lives. The most important thing is that they should be certain that they will not regret their decision, even if they have no or little success.

sections.label: Part B
sections.instructions: Match the paragraphs with the topics. There is ONE extra topic.

options.text: A University degree does not guarantee a happy and wealthy life
The quickest and easiest way to be rich
What is the argument about?
The importance of being a devoted worker
Taking initiative brings success
The meaning of success is unique for each individual

sections.label: Part C
sections.instructions: Match the sentences (a–h) with the gaps (1–6) in the passage. There are TWO extra sentences.

options.text: They will likely be better off with a degree than without one.
If that person owns a business, the rewards are often higher than he would get from a job.
People who are more self-confident and determined are more likely to be successful at their own businesses.
What's more, countless others have gone on to start successful businesses without first earning a bachelor's degree.
Regardless of whether they have a degree, most people would like to be financially well-off.
Some may consider themselves successful if they are being respected by their peers.
There is no absolute answer that will give people a clear direction to follow.
Whether they have a degree or not does not have a decisive role, but they may not succeed at first.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT MENTIONED in paragraph 1?

options.text: There are many examples of successful people without university degrees.
It is still argued if a university degree is essential or not.
There are unemployed people with high qualifications.
University degrees and qualifications enable you to be more advantageous.

items.question: According to paragraph 2, which of the following is NOT necessary to be successful?

options.text: Working at the same place for years
Learning from one's mistakes
Being a dedicated worker
Showing effort

items.question: According to paragraph 3, who may lose focus and never experience the success of their actions?

options.text: A person who owns a business
Someone who needs to be told what to do
Someone who has only a bachelor's degree
A person who works for a company

items.question: Which of the following is NOT given as a definition of success in paragraph 5?

options.text: Having a loving and caring family
Being able to afford to buy the things you want
Following your path and ambitions
Being appreciated by others

items.question: What does the writer highlight in paragraph 5?

options.text: How you define success describes your goals.
The desire to be independent brings success.
You should never feel guilty about your decisions.
Having no or little success makes you more ambitious.

sections.label: Part E
sections.instructions: Guess the meaning of the words from the text. They are bold in the passage.

items.question: "dropout" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to:

options.text: a student who leaves school or university before completely finishing
a student who becomes successful without a university degree
a student who gets extremely wealthy
a student who has enthusiasm and goals to be successful

items.question: "endeavors" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to:

options.text: risks
mistakes
dreams
attempts

items.question: "decent" in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to:

options.text: appropriate
part-time
low paid
boring

items.question: "a safer bet" in paragraph 4 is closest in meaning to:

options.text: less beneficial
a sensible choice
a happier life
a better income

items.question: "assure" in paragraph 5 is closest in meaning to:

options.text: complete
restrict
monitor
guarantee

items.question: "pursue" in paragraph 5 is closest in meaning to:

options.text: experiment
deprive
follow
provide

sections.label: Part F
sections.instructions: What do you think about the following? (write at least 50 words)
sections.prompt: "A university degree is a must to be successful in life."

passages.title: Brand Stretching
passages.introHtml: Before you read, think about what brand stretching might mean.
passages.passageHtml:
A manufacturer of sports shoes starts selling consumer electronics. A soft drink lends its name to a range of urban clothing. What's going on? In simpler times, you knew where you were with brands. Today, however, big companies try to redefine brands not so much as a product but more as a way of life, and they want to stretch them into new areas. In the early years of consumer society, a brand name on a box simply identified what was inside. People were looking for products that would improve their quality of life, and choose brands most likely to achieve that purpose. The functionality of the product was still important, but people also started using brands to say something about themselves. For example, choosing a brand of cosmetics would suggest they belonged to high society.

Now, we have entered the third age of branding, in which so many companies are making roughly the same product at roughly the same price. Instead, companies are trying to make their brands stand out by emphasising their emotional aspects, hoping consumers will identify with the set of values the brand represents.

One disadvantage of a product-based brand is that if the product goes out of fashion, the brand goes with it. This is a serious concern for manufacturers of breakfast cereals, who are struggling with weak demand for their products. The advantage of emotional brands is that companies can transfer their brand strength into other areas. This way they can increase revenues and reduce their exposure to the lifespan of a single product.

In addition to this, brands are flexible. On the one hand, they can have solid and practical values; on the other hand, they can have more abstract or emotional values. An example of a practical brand would be one related to a transport network such as a train company. In this instance, the brand is associated with infrastructure and its ability to get you from A to B. An example of a more abstract or emotional brand would be a leisure brand such as Nike that associates itself with dreams and enjoyment. It is the latter which has the maximum potential for stretch.

But even emotional brands have a limit to their flexibility. The product has to be related to the brand promise. From this viewpoint, it made sense when a sports shoe company moved into consumer electronics by stretching their brand to include walkie-talkies for hikers because it was sports-focused.

When the move was made from soft drinks into clothing, however, the branding consultants were not very excited. The drink was so closely associated with that particular brand name that moving into clothing from the drink was mentally difficult and quite risky. However, the emotional attributes that youngsters find appealing in the drink are fashionable at the moment. Maybe this just shows that an inspired move — even if risky — sometimes pays off.

sections.label: Part B
sections.instructions: Match the paragraphs with the headings. There is ONE extra heading.

options.text: An unexpected success
Brands encouraging overconsumption
How industrialization can change consumer attitudes to brands
Functionality no longer as the focus of marketing
One drawback of a product-based brand
The kinds of brands that are more likely to stretch
An important thing to consider when brand stretching

sections.label: Part C
sections.instructions: Match the sentences (a–h) with the gaps (1–6). There are TWO extra sentences.

options.text: But as people in industrialised nations became wealthier and fulfilled their needs, brands acquired a different meaning.
Brand recognition is also important in marketing.
So far, their marketing efforts seem to be having little effect.
One brand name meant good-quality sports shoes, another a soft drink; no confusion.
Therefore, the response to the clothes with the same name as the drink has been positive.
Just to sell a product with your logo on it is a short-term, bad idea.
Brands become more successful when companies consider the needs and expectancies of their customers.
In other words, there is not much difference between products in terms of functionality.

sections.label: Part D
sections.instructions: Choose the best option according to the text.

items.question: According to paragraph 1, what led to a change in attitudes to brands?

options.text: the influence of new products on the market
the financial circumstances of consumers
more sophisticated marketing
a greater choice of products

items.question: According to paragraph 2, which of the following is true about the third age of branding?

options.text: Competing products may function equally well.
The range of products available is too large to survive.
Consumers do not change their favourite products.
Price has become a key factor in consumers' choice of products.

items.question: In paragraph 3, the writer mentions manufacturers of breakfast cereals to show how _____.

options.text: competition can have an impact on a product
a brand can lose its popularity despite a high demand for the product
without advertising a product cannot be sold
changes in the popularity of products can cause difficulties

items.question: In paragraph 4, the writer refers to railways to show that brands like this _____.

options.text: do not recognise the value of stretching
suffer from having an unattractive image
are not suitable for stretching
are extremely difficult to advertise

items.question: In paragraph 5, the writer argues that the stretch from sports shoes into consumer electronics is likely to be successful because _____.

options.text: existing customers have demanded the new products
they will be sold in the same outlets
the cost of the new products will be the same
there is a connection in the way that the goods can be used

items.question: In paragraph 6, the writer argues that the stretch from soft drinks into clothing _____.

options.text: was a gamble that succeeded
showed the value of careful advertising
created production problems for the manufacturer
did not appeal to young people

items.question: In paragraph 1, 'they' refers to ____.

options.text: brands
consumer electronics
companies
products

items.question: In paragraph 3, 'it' refers to ____.

options.text: disadvantage
product
brand
fashion

items.question: In paragraph 4, 'latter' refers to ____.

options.text: transport network
dreams and enjoyment
practical brand
emotional brand

items.question: In paragraph 6, 'associated with' refers to ____.

options.text: in the centre of
connected with
faced with
deal with

sections.label: Part E
sections.instructions: Find a word in the text which means the following. Type it in the box.

items.definition: the amount of time that a person lives or a thing exists
existing as an idea, feeling, or quality, rather than as something real that you can see or touch
a way of thinking about a situation
to be advantageous and yield benefits

sections.label: Part F
sections.instructions: Answer the following question in your own words (minimum 50 words).
sections.prompt: What is more important to you in a product: functionality, price, or its brand? Why?

passages.title: Getting the Picture from DNA
passages.subtitle: Working out what someone looks like from only a DNA sample is no longer science fiction. You'd be surprised what forensics experts can already do, says Clare Wilson.
passages.passageHtml:
At present, if police find DNA which could be that of the criminal at the scene of a crime (for example, in blood or hair), standard forensic techniques can help in two ways. If there's a suspect in custody, the police can see if their DNA matches the 'crime stain', as it is called. Or in the absence of a suspect, they can see if it matches the DNA of any known criminal held in their archives.

Both techniques have proved their worth in criminal investigations. Ever since DNA testing was introduced, forensic scientists have wondered how much a DNA sample on its own could tell them about what a criminal might look like.

Scientists have already had some success with predicting hair colour from DNA samples. For example, researchers at Britain's Forensic Science Service (FSS) have developed a DNA test which will tell with 98% accuracy whether or not someone has red hair. However, the red-hair test is of limited use in Britain, where only 6% of the population are red-headed. What about blonde, brown and black-haired criminals? Hair colour is usually determined by the cumulative effect of several genes, so unfortunately there's no such thing as a single gene for blonde hair that could be turned into a simple test, for example. It's the same with eye colour.

But biotechnology firm DNAPrint Genomics of Florida, USA, is having a crack at both problems. As a starting point, research was carried out using mice to discover the genes that controlled eye colour. Next, the DNAPrint researchers took DNA samples from 500 volunteers and recorded their eye colours. They then applied a technique called SNP mapping to see if they could discover any correlations between eye colour and DNA. Of the ten genes, they found that only four really matter. By looking at these, they can classify someone as having dark eyes (black and brown), light coloured eyes (blue and grey), or hazel eyes (greenish-brown) — with 97% certainty.

DNAPrint is now applying exactly the same technique to hair colour, identifying possible genes and looking for SNPs. Representatives say they have made some headway and can classify people into one of three groups — blonde, brown or black-haired — with some accuracy, from their DNA alone.

Back in Britain, the Forensic Science Service has also pursued the genetic basis of facial features. A few years ago, it helped fund a major project carried out by scientists at University College London (UCL). Over several months, an exhibit at a London museum invited visitors to leave DNA samples and have their faces scanned using 3D surface mapping. The UCL researchers tried to break down this data on overall facial shape into distinct features such as the curve of the nose or chin clefts and correlate them to DNA sequences. But they made little progress. When the lead scientist retired, the project ended without drawing any firm conclusions.

But the idea of finding genes for facial features isn't dead. Many of the genes involved are common to most mammals. One promising project has found that mice show significant variation in jaw shape and size, and has begun to unfold the genetics behind the variation. Project leader Chris Killgenberg of the University of Konstanz in Germany cautions that, as with humans, the genetics controlling jaw shape in mice is horribly complicated, but the project is making some progress. In one study of 535 mice, it has identified genes for jaw shape, jaw size and jaw symmetry and found two basic patterns resulting from the combination of these genes.

The UK-based human rights group known as 'Liberty' has concerns, saying that the existing tests are not yet sufficiently conclusive to be used as a basis for arresting suspects. Kevin Sullivan, from the Forensic Science Service, points out that when it comes to someone's facial characteristics, playing rugby might have more of an effect on your ear and nose shape than your genes. But he is optimistic about the future of the research. 'Law-abiding citizens don't have anything to worry about,' he says. 'But criminals do.'

sections.label: Part A
sections.instructions: Match the paragraphs with the headings. There is ONE extra heading.

options.text: A promising study which aims to find the genetic basis for facial characteristics
Doubts about the efficiency of DNA testing to catch criminals
A failed attempt in finding the genes for facial features
Research into the genetic basis of eye colour
Using DNA testing to prove the innocence of arrested suspects
Limitation of DNA testing to predict hair colour
Progress in predicting hair colour correctly

sections.label: Part B
sections.instructions: Match the sentences (a–h) with the gaps (1–6). There are TWO extra sentences.

options.text: However, the findings weren't in line with the expected outcome.
Certainly, genes never tell the whole story with physical characteristics — environment plays a key role too.
About 600 people volunteered.
So, a gene for a large jaw in mice, for example, might very well be found in humans, too.
Similar areas in human DNA were then investigated, and ten possible genes were found.
But what if there's no suspect and no match in the archive?
Just as with eye colour, there is no one gene for a big nose, so the enormous complexity of the task defeated the researchers.
It was found that the genes in humans and animals work in a slightly similar way.

sections.label: Part C
sections.instructions: Choose the best answer according to the text.

items.question: In paragraph 1, what is meant by a 'crime stain'?

options.text: too much blood at the scene of a crime
DNA belonging to known criminals
samples of blood or hair in criminal archives
DNA samples left at the scene of a crime

items.question: Forensic scientists are interested in finding out _____.

options.text: if the genes responsible for criminal tendencies can be identified
how far personal appearance can be predicted from DNA
if hair colour could be linked to criminal behaviour
whether or not DNA can be used to identify a suspected criminal

items.question: What problem do scientists face in developing DNA tests for hair and eye colour?

options.text: the fact that hair and eye colour are not generally determined by one gene
finding a sufficient number of volunteers to take DNA samples from
the fact that hair and eye colour are not necessarily related
the distribution of hair and eye colour from one country to another

items.question: According to paragraph 6, the project carried out by UCL failed because _____.

options.text: the lead scientist retired
the funds were insufficient
the researchers were incompetent
the task proved unmanageable

items.question: We can understand from paragraph 7 that the research being done on mice aims to _____.

options.text: find out about genes for facial features
identify different jaw shapes in mice
compare and contrast the jaw shapes of different mice
prove the complexity of genes associated with facial features

items.question: According to paragraph 8, the human rights group, 'Liberty', has concerns because they think that _____.

options.text: the environment plays a minor role in turning people into criminals
the stories of the suspects are as important as their physical characteristics
the DNA tests do not yet provide totally accurate results
the police don't take DNA tests into consideration when arresting suspects

items.question: The expression 'have a crack at' in paragraph 4 is closest in meaning to ___.

options.text: pose a threat
make a decision
make an attempt
take an opportunity

items.question: The expression 'make headway' in paragraph 5 is closest in meaning to ___.

options.text: make a difference
make progress
make a model
make a discovery

sections.label: Part D
sections.instructions: Find a word in the text which means the following. Type it in the box.

items.definition: scientific techniques used for solving crimes
the quality of being true, correct or exact
a connection between two ideas, facts etc, especially when one may be the cause of the other

sections.label: Part E
sections.instructions: What do the following words refer to? They are bold and underlined in the text.

items.label: these (paragraph 4)
acceptable: the four genes that really matter

sections.label: Part F
sections.instructions: Answer the following question in your own words (minimum 50 words).
sections.prompt: Do you think it is right to use DNA samples in criminal investigations? Why? / Why not?
